text: The surgeon begins the operation by dissecting the gastrohepatic ligament. The left gastric artery is visible overlying the operative field. The surgeon carefully skeletonizes the artery, placing four clips for hemostatic control before transecting it between the clips using a harmonic scalpel.

With the left gastric artery displaced, the surgeon identifies the right crus and separates its pillar from the esophagus using careful sweeping. The dissection continues over the anterior esophagus to the left crus pillar, exposing four to five centimeters of the esophagus below the diaphragm. A myotomy is performed over a six-centimeter segment of the esophagus and a two to three-centimeter segment of the gastric wall. The surgeon then focuses on the mid-body of the greater curve of the stomach, transecting the greater omentum and short gastric arteries to mobilize the stomach fundus for fundoplication. The fat pad at the gastroesophageal junction is divided, revealing the dissection plane.

The surgeon begins the esophagocardiomyotomy at the eleven o'clock position on the esophagus, approximately three to four centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. Using the blade of the harmonic scalpel, the longitudinal and circular muscle fibers of the esophagus are carefully divided while preserving the deeper mucosal layer. As the dissection progresses distally towards the thicker cardia of the stomach, a hook cautery is employed to pull muscle fibers away from the mucosal layer, preventing thermal injury. The circular muscle fibers become visible as they are further divided, and proximal fibers are transected with the harmonic scalpel. Near the gastroesophageal junction, the lower esophageal sphincter fibers are adherent to the mucosal layer, requiring meticulous dissection with fine-tipped scissors.

The surgeon performs an anterior partial fundoplication by placing three sets of sutures. The first set involves the fundus of the stomach, the left cruce, and the muscle layers of the esophagus. The second set incorporates the fundus and the rim of the esophageal hiatus, while the final set involves the fundus and the right pillar of the cruce.

The average patient will remain in the hospital for one night after the operation. On the morning of post-operative day one, it is my preference to obtain a gastrographin swallow study to ensure that there is no leak from the esophagus. When the fundoplication is complete, all needles are removed, all suture material is removed, and the liver retractor is disengaged under direct visualization and the operation is complete.
